items.html:
Who is an adult at risk?

Safeguarding does not apply to every person you support in the same way. The Care Act 2014 describes an adult at risk as someone aged 18 or over who has needs for care and support, is experiencing or is at risk of abuse or neglect, and — because of those needs — is unable to protect themselves from it.

All three parts have to be present. Someone might have care needs yet be well able to keep themselves safe; someone else may face abuse but have no care needs. It is the combination that makes a person an adult at risk, so the same individual can be at risk in one situation and not in another.

Making Safeguarding Personal

Making Safeguarding Personal is the approach that keeps the person, not the process, at the centre. Rather than doing safeguarding “to” people, you work with them to find out what they want to change and what a good outcome looks like for them.

  • Ask, do not assume: begin with “what would you like to happen?” rather than deciding for them.
  • Respect their wishes: an adult with capacity may choose how far a concern is taken forward.
  • Focus on outcomes: success is measured by the person feeling safer and more in control.
  • Stay proportionate: the response should fit the situation and be no more intrusive than it needs to be.

items.question: Under the Care Act 2014, which combination describes an adult at risk?

answers.text: An adult with care and support needs who is experiencing or at risk of abuse or neglect and cannot protect themselves because of those needs.
answers.feedback: Correct. All three elements — care needs, abuse or the risk of it, and being unable to self-protect — must be present together.

answers.text: Any adult over 65, because older people are always vulnerable.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Age alone does not make someone an adult at risk; it depends on care needs and the situation.

answers.text: Any adult who has a disability, whatever their circumstances.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Having a disability does not automatically make a person an adult at risk; the Care Act test still has to be met.

answers.text: An adult who is unhappy with their care but has no care and support needs.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Without care and support needs the Care Act definition is not met, although concerns should still be listened to.

items.html:
Abuse that can be easy to miss

Beyond the broad categories you have already met, some forms of abuse have particular warning signs. Domestic abuse is any controlling, coercive, threatening, violent or abusive behaviour between people who are, or have been, intimate partners or family members. It is not only physical — controlling money, isolating someone from friends, and constant belittling are all part of it.

Modern slavery and human trafficking involve exploiting a person for labour, domestic servitude, sexual exploitation or crime. Signs can include someone who has no identity documents, seems fearful of a controlling companion, works excessive hours, or keeps none of their own earnings.

Self-neglect, organisational and discriminatory abuse

Not all harm comes from another person. Self-neglect is where an adult neglects their own health, hygiene or surroundings, or hoards to a degree that puts them at risk. It still falls within safeguarding and needs a caring, person-led response rather than judgement.

  • Organisational abuse: neglect or poor care that comes from the routines, culture or practices of a service — for example rigid rotas that ignore people’s choices.
  • Discriminatory abuse: harassment or unequal treatment because of a protected characteristic such as race, religion, disability, age, sex or sexual orientation.
  • Overlap: a person may experience more than one type at once, so keep an open mind.

items.question: Self-neglect, where an adult neglects their own health, hygiene or surroundings, can be a safeguarding matter even though no other person is causing the harm.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Self-neglect is recognised within adult safeguarding and calls for a supportive, person-centred response.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Self-neglect is included within adult safeguarding; the harm does not have to come from another person for a concern to apply.

items.html:
When someone tells you about abuse

A disclosure is one of the most important moments in safeguarding, and how you react shapes whether the person feels believed. Stay calm, give them your full attention, and let them speak in their own words. You are there to listen and to keep them safe, not to investigate.

Above all, never promise to keep a disclosure secret. It can be tempting to reassure someone by saying it will stay between the two of you, but you have a duty to pass concerns on. Instead, explain gently that you will need to tell the right person so they can get help.

The do’s and don’ts

  • Do reassure the person that they were right to tell you and that it is not their fault.
  • Do record what was said as soon as you can, using the person’s own words and the facts.
  • Do preserve any evidence — avoid washing, tidying or moving things where a crime may have happened.
  • Do not ask leading questions, press for detail, or interrogate the person.
  • Do not confront the alleged abuser or discuss the concern with them.
  • Do not delay — report to your manager or the safeguarding lead straight away.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete these key points about responding to a disclosure.
items.textField: When someone discloses abuse you should stay *calm* and listen carefully. You must never promise to keep it *secret*, because you have a duty to pass the concern on. Record what was said using the person’s own *words* as soon as you can. Avoid asking *leading* questions that put ideas into their account. Where a crime may have occurred, take care to preserve any *evidence*.

items.html:
Reporting and recording

Once a concern is raised, it must reach the people who can act on it. In your organisation this means telling your manager or safeguarding lead without delay, and following your local safeguarding policy. If you believe someone is in immediate danger, or a serious crime is taking place, call 999 first.

Good recording supports everything that follows. Write down the facts promptly, separate what you saw from what you were told, avoid opinion, and sign and date your notes. Clear, factual records help agencies build an accurate picture and protect the person.

Multi-agency working

Safeguarding adults is a shared responsibility, and no single organisation holds all the answers. Several partners work together, each with a distinct role.

  • The local authority: leads adult safeguarding and decides whether a formal enquiry is needed.
  • The police: investigate where a crime may have been committed.
  • The CQC: regulates care services and acts on concerns about the quality and safety of care.
  • Safeguarding Adults Boards: bring partners together to agree how agencies protect adults locally and to learn from serious cases.

items.question: Which organisation has the lead role in adult safeguarding and decides whether a formal enquiry should take place?

answers.feedback: Correct. Under the Care Act 2014 the local authority leads adult safeguarding and decides whether an enquiry is needed.
Incorrect. The police lead on criminal investigations, but the local authority leads the safeguarding response overall.
Incorrect. The CQC regulates services, but it is not the lead agency for individual safeguarding enquiries.
Incorrect. Your organisation must report and cooperate, but the local authority holds the lead safeguarding role.

items.html:
The Care Act 2014 and section 42

The Care Act 2014 gives adult safeguarding its legal foundation in England. Section 42 places a duty on the local authority to make enquiries, or to have someone else make them, whenever it has reasonable cause to suspect that an adult with care and support needs is experiencing, or is at risk of, abuse or neglect and cannot protect themselves.

A section 42 enquiry finds out what has happened and what should be done to keep the person safe, always guided by their own views and desired outcomes. Your reports and records are often what set this duty in motion.

A realistic scenario

You visit Doris, who has care needs after a stroke. You notice unexplained bruising, her purse is often empty before the end of the week, and a relative who manages her money has begun turning visitors away. Doris seems anxious and hints that she is “not allowed” to spend her own money.

This raises possible physical and financial abuse. You do not confront the relative or investigate. You reassure Doris, record the facts in her words, and report to your safeguarding lead the same day so the local authority can consider a section 42 enquiry.

items.question: In the scenario, what is the right thing for you to do about your concerns for Doris?

answers.text: Reassure Doris, record the facts in her own words, and report to your safeguarding lead the same day.
answers.feedback: Correct. Prompt, factual reporting lets the local authority consider a section 42 enquiry while keeping Doris at the centre.

answers.text: Ask the relative directly whether they are taking Doris’s money.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Confronting the alleged abuser can place Doris at greater risk and may interfere with any investigation.

answers.text: Wait a few weeks to see whether the bruising and missing money happen again.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Delay leaves Doris at risk; concerns should be reported without waiting for more incidents.

answers.text: Decide it is a family matter and none of your business.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. These are possible signs of abuse of an adult at risk, and you have a duty to report them.
